reference_groups.name: Probably referred to by David in Ps 55:12-14

ai_enrichment.summary: Ahithophel foi um conselheiro de Davi, conhecido por sua sabedoria e astúcia. Durante a revolta de Absalão, ele desertou Davi e se uniu a Absalão, oferecendo conselhos estratégicos que, embora sábios, foram contrabalançados por Hushai. Quando percebeu que sua influência havia diminuído, Ahithophel retornou a sua casa em Giloh, onde se suicidou. Sua história é um exemplo de traição e desespero, refletindo temas de lealdade e a fragilidade da sabedoria humana.
outline: Ahithophel como conselheiro de Davi: sua sabedoria e influência (2 Samuel 16:23)
A traição de Ahithophel: aliança com Absalão durante a revolta (2 Samuel 15:12)
O conselho de Ahithophel e a intervenção de Hushai: a luta pelo poder (2 Samuel 15:31-37)
O desespero de Ahithophel: sua decisão de suicídio após a rejeição (2 Samuel 17:1-23)
A conexão de Ahithophel com a linhagem de Davi: avô de Bate-Seba (2 Samuel 11:3; 2 Samuel 23:34)
The Role of Counsel in Leadership (2 Samuel 15:31; 2 Samuel 16:23)
The Impact of Rebellion on Relationships (2 Samuel 15:12; 2 Samuel 17:1)
ai_enrichment.theological_significance: A história de Ahithophel ilustra a complexidade da sabedoria humana e a fragilidade das alianças. Sua traição e subsequente suicídio refletem a consequência do afastamento de Deus e a busca por poder a qualquer custo. Este relato serve como um aviso sobre a importância da lealdade e da confiança em Deus, em vez de depender da sabedoria humana.

ai_themes_normalized.aspect: impacto da traição na vida de Ahithophel
contraste entre a sabedoria de Ahithophel e a intervenção divina

original_theme.label_original: A soberania de Deus sobre a história

definitions.text: Brother of insipidity or impiety, a man greatly renowned for his sagacity among the Jews. At the time of Absalom’s revolt he deserted David (Ps. 41:9; 55:12-14) and espoused the cause of Absalom (2 Sam. 15:12). David sent his old friend Hushai back to Absalom, in order that he might counteract the counsel of Ahithophel (2 Sam. 15:31-37). This end was so far gained that Ahithophel saw he had no longer any influence, and accordingly he at once left the camp of Absalom and returned to Giloh, his native place, where, after arranging his wordly affairs, he hanged himself, and was buried in the sepulchre of his fathers (2 Sam. 17:1-23). He was the type of Judas (Ps. 41:9).

(brother of foolishness), a native of Giloh, was a privy councillor of David, whose wisdom was highly esteemed, though his name had an exactly opposite signification. (2 Samuel 16:23) (B.C. 1055-1023.) He was the grandfather of Bathsheba. Comp. (2 Samuel 11:3) with 2Sam 23:34 Ahithophel joined the conspiracy of Absalom against David, and persuaded him to take possession of the royal harem, (2 Samuel 16:21) and recommended an immediate pursuit of David. His advice was wise; but Hushai advised otherwise. When Ahithophel saw that Hushai’s advice prevailed, he despaired of success, and returning to his own home “put his household in order and hanged himself.” (2 Samuel 17:1-23)

ai_entities.context: rei de Israel e alvo da traição de Ahithophel

ai_discovered_aspects.label: The Role of Counsel in Leadership
ai_discovered_aspects.rationale: Ahithophel's role as a counselor highlights the importance of wise counsel in leadership, which is often overlooked in discussions about his betrayal.

ai_discovered_aspects.label: The Impact of Rebellion on Relationships
ai_discovered_aspects.rationale: The rebellion of Absalom not only affected David but also had profound implications for his advisors, showcasing how political strife can fracture personal relationships.

ai_discovered_aspects.label: Legacy of Betrayal
ai_discovered_aspects.rationale: Ahithophel's legacy as a traitor serves as a cautionary tale about the long-term consequences of betrayal, influencing future generations.

ai_relationships.description: Ahithophel foi um conselheiro próximo de Davi antes de sua traição.
Ahithophel se uniu a Absalão durante sua revolta contra Davi.
Ahithophel é o avô de Bate-Seba, mãe de Salomão.
